critic_raw:
This dermatoscopic CNN experiment has four methodological flaws that could inflate or mischaracterize the performance advantage.

1. Normalization leakage via full-dataset statistics. The design states: 'all continuous features are standardized using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' This is data leakage. Validation and test set pixels inform the mean and standard deviation used to normalize training images. In practice the magnitude of this leakage is small for large datasets with stable pixel distributions, but it is explicitly prohibited by sound experimental design and contaminates the holdout evaluation. The design acknowledges this under 'leakage prevention' with the justification 'provides more reliable normalization as it avoids instability from small training sets' — but with 6,000 training images, instability is not a credible concern. Evidence: refit normalization statistics on training set only and report whether test metrics change by more than the bootstrap confidence interval. If they do not, the leakage had no material effect and can be dismissed; if they do, the reported comparison is compromised.

2. Selective filtering on high inter-rater agreement inflates performance and limits generalizability. Training and evaluation are restricted to images with >90% inter-rater agreement. This removes the hardest diagnostic cases — exactly the cases where learned features would most plausibly diverge from handcrafted features. A CNN trained and evaluated only on 'easy' images (high agreement) may show weaker advantage than one evaluated on the full distribution, or may show inflated advantage because the task has been made artificially tractable. The claim of CNN superiority over traditional features is materially different in scope depending on whether it holds for the ambiguous 10% of cases. Evidence: report per-agreement-tier performance separately; if CNN advantage disappears or reverses in the low-agreement tier, the scope of the claim must be narrowed significantly.

3. Comparison is CNN vs. logistic regression, not CNN vs. best traditional pipeline. The baseline is logistic regression on handcrafted features. This is a weak baseline for dermatology image classification. A gradient-boosted tree ensemble on the same handcrafted features routinely outperforms logistic regression by 5-10 F1 points on medical imaging tasks. The paper's justification — that random forest would 'conflate feature engineering with model complexity' — is backwards: the hypothesis is about learned vs. handcrafted features, so comparing against the best handcrafted-feature model is correct. If logistic regression on handcrafted features is the only baseline, a CNN win may reflect the deficiency of the linear classifier, not the superiority of learned features. Evidence: add a random forest or gradient boosting baseline on identical handcrafted features. If the gap between CNN and this stronger baseline is substantially smaller, the conclusion changes.

4. Sensitivity analysis label states inter-rater agreement cutoff as '>80%' in the controls section but '>90%' in the data section. This inconsistency means either the reported filtering is different from the analysis filtering, or the sensitivity analysis uses a different population than the main analysis. If these populations differ substantially in class distribution or difficulty, comparing results across them is not a straightforward sensitivity analysis. Evidence: clarify and reconcile the two thresholds; confirm the sensitivity analysis uses the same data pipeline as the main analysis.
all_issues_raised: Normalization statistics computed on full dataset before splitting — explicit data leakage into validation and test sets
Restricting to high inter-rater agreement (>90%) removes hardest cases, inflating apparent performance and limiting generalizability of CNN advantage claim
Baseline is logistic regression on handcrafted features — insufficient; gradient boosting or random forest on same features is the appropriate strong baseline
Inconsistency between '>90%' threshold in data section and '>80%' in sensitivity analysis section — conflicting populations
High inter-rater agreement selection bias: training and evaluation restricted to easy cases, performance on hard/ambiguous cases in deployment is unknown
Stratified random split permits temporal leakage; temporal drift from device and protocol changes is not addressed
Normalization statistics computed on full dataset before splitting constitutes distributional leakage from test set
No patient-level isolation: multiple images per patient may appear across splits, inflating performance via patient-specific feature memorization
Traditional baseline omits pretrained deep features, conflating end-to-end fine-tuning advantage with CNN architectural advantage
Normalization statistics fit on full dataset before splitting constitutes preprocessing leakage into test set
Training and evaluation data restricted to high inter-rater agreement images excludes hard cases, making claims non-generalizable to clinical practice
Asymmetric model selection: CNN fine-tuning strategy not treated as hyperparameter while baseline regularization is grid-searched
Class weighting combined with macro F1 optimization creates implicit threshold tuning that inflates validation-set metric
